title: practical english usage - Part 134
content:
Few of them told the truth.

Few of the people questioned told the truth.

- Few people always tell the truth.

Could 1 have a little of that?

Could I have a little of that butter?

- Could I have a little butter?

(a) little of and (a) few of Compare: Before another determiner (article, possessive, etc) or a pronoun, we  use Determiners:       Quantifiers        Section  13 169 grammar •        less and fewer For other uses of least, see the Index.

especially in an informal style. Some people feel this is incorrect.

Least is often used instead of fewest before plural nouns (... the least mistakes), The translation with the fewest mistakes isn't always the best.

The fewest can be used before plural nouns as the superlative of few (►   168).

3  the    fewest        as  determiner:       superlative        of  few ' What's the time?' ‘ 1 haven't got the least idea.' Do you think there's the least chance of Smith winning the election?

With singular abstract nouns, the least can mean 'any ... at all'.

2 'any   ... at  all' ' What will your mother think? ‘ That's the least of my worries.' We   use the least of before plural abstract nouns to mean 'the smallest of.

Jess earns the most money in our family; Dan earns the least.

The least can be used without a noun if the meaning is clear.

I think I probably do the least work in this office.

superlative of little (= not much, ►    168), and the opposite of the most.

The least can be used as a determiner before uncountable nouns; it is the 1  the    least      as  determiner:       superlative        of  little 170 least      and   fewest For   leaner. ►  5 HL I worry less than I used to.

Less can be used as an adverb (the opposite of the adverb more).

Some people go to church, but less/fewer than 20 years ago.

Nouns can be dropped after less and fewer if the meaning is clear.

3 less     and   fewer       without      nouns At the college reunions, there are fewer of us each year.

- I'd like to spend less of my time answering emails.

Fewer people make their own bread these days, (     Fewer-of people...) not - If you want to lose weight, eat less food, (     ... less ef-food.) and fewer of. Compare: Before another determiner (article, possessive, etc) or a pronoun, we  use less of 2  less/fewer          with    and   without      of I've got less problems than I used to have.

consider this incorrect.

In an informal style, less is quite common before plural nouns. Some people / earn less money than a cleaner. I've got fewer problems than I used to have.

Fewer is the comparative of few (used before plural nouns). Compare: Less is the comparative of little (used especially before uncountable nouns).

169 less     and   fewer grammar »171     enough You didn't buy enough of them.

- You didn't buy enough cards, (     ... enough of cards) We haven't got enough of that blue paint.

- We haven't got enough blue paint, (     . .  enough pftbiue point;) I don't understand enough of the words in the notice.

- I don't know enough Spanish to read this, (     .. .-enough of Spanish...) enough of. Compare: Before another determiner (article, possessive, etc) or a pronoun, we  use If only we had time enough ...  I was fool enough to believe him.

except in a few expressions.

Enough is occasionally used after a noun, but this is rare in modern English Have you got enough milk?  There aren 't enough glasses.

Enough can be used before a noun as a determiner.

1 enough        + noun 171 enough For less and fewer, ►     169.

I was not in the least upset by her bad temper.

talking about personal feelings and reactions.

We   can use not in the least in a formal style to mean 'not at all', especially when 7 not    in   the    least We lost everything in the fire. But at least nobody was hurt.

thing is certain or all right, even if everything else is unsatisfactory.

We   can also use at least as a discourse marker (►  284.3) to suggest that one He's been in love at least eight times this year.

'How old do you think he is? 'At least thirty.' At least means 'not less than (but perhaps more than)'.
